name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC designs, builds, and operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, capturing daily imagery of the entire planet at 3.5-meter resolution via its SuperDove constellation. The company also operates SkySat and Pelican high-resolution satellites (50cm) and Tanager hyperspectral imaging satellites. Planet delivers geospatial data and analytics through its cloud-based platform to agriculture, mapping, energy, forestry, finance, insurance, and government customers. Founded in 2010, headquartered in San Francisco.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet launched its next-generation Pelican satellite constellation in 2024-2025 with 30cm resolution capability, a significant upgrade from the 3.5m SuperDove fleet, and deployed the Tanager hyperspectral satellite in partnership with Carbon Mapper for methane/CO2 detection — opening an entirely new environmental monitoring revenue stream [1][2]. AI Deployment (2/2): Planet has repositioned itself as a 'Space and AI' company, shipping AI-powered analytics products including automated change detection, crop monitoring, and infrastructure monitoring tools that generate quantifiable revenue from its data platform — FY2026 data-as-a-service revenue grew significantly as customers increasingly purchased analytics, not just imagery [1][2]. Market Share (1/2): Planet operates the largest commercial Earth observation satellite fleet (200+ satellites) and is the leading daily-cadence imaging provider, though it competes with Maxar, Airbus Defence & Space, and Capella Space for high-resolution contracts [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Pelican constellation expansion is on track with specific deployment timelines through 2027, and FY2027 guidance of $415-440M revenue represents 35-43% growth, providing strong visibility [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending is substantial relative to Planet's $308M revenue base, typical for a hardware-software hybrid space company investing in next-gen satellite and AI analytics capabilities [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's mission — 'to accelerate humanity toward a more sustainable, secure world by illuminating environmental and social change' — is specific, thoughtful, and clearly connected to the company's satellite imaging capabilities [2]. As a Public Benefit Corporation (PBC), Planet has a legal charter to balance stakeholder and shareholder interests. The mission shapes product decisions including pro-bono disaster response imagery and the Carbon Mapper partnership. However, the mission has not yet been validated across multi-decade cycles or leadership transitions — Planet was founded in 2010 and CEO Will Marshall has led since inception. Community Impact (1/2): Planet provides free satellite imagery during humanitarian crises and natural disasters, partnered with Global Heritage Fund for cultural heritage protection in Ukraine, and maintains ongoing relationships with NGOs and academic institutions for environmental monitoring [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): Glassdoor reviews highlight Planet attracting 'brilliant, kind, and compassionate' problem-solvers, with the CEO and executive team described as committed to transparency, though scaling challenges and management inconsistencies have been noted [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Will Marshall and co-founders maintain significant equity stakes through the dual-class share structure, demonstrating long-term alignment, though no notable net insider buying was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. FY2026 (ending Jan 2026) revenue of $307.7M grew 26% YoY with Q4 hitting $86.8M (41% YoY growth), and FY2027 guidance of $415-440M implies 35-43% acceleration [1]. Planet achieved its first full fiscal year of non-GAAP profitability with $15.5M adjusted EBITDA and generated $234M free cash flow. However, GAAP net loss widened to -$247M (profit margin -80.2%) driven by stock-based compensation and non-cash charges. D/E of 245% reflects $462M debt against $640M cash. ROE is deeply negative at -78.4% due to accumulated losses. The strong top-line growth (41% YoY, 17.2% 3-year CAGR) offsets weakness in profitability metrics, but unprofitable operations and high leverage cap the infrastructure score [1][4]. Calculation: D/E 245% (0.24, 1x) + ROE -78.4% (0.00, 2x) + Rev YoY 41.1% (1.00, 1x) + Rev CAGR 17.2% (1.00, 2x) + Fwd P/E negative (0.00, 1x) + Margin -80.2% (0.00, 1x) = 3.24/8 = 4.0/10.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Planet's core business — designing, launching, and operating physical satellite constellations — requires aerospace engineering, orbital mechanics, and manufacturing capabilities that AI cannot substitute [1]. The satellite hardware and orbital infrastructure create a structural moat. However, Planet's downstream analytics business faces competitive pressure from AI-native geospatial analytics startups and large cloud providers (Google Earth Engine, Microsoft Planetary Computer) that could build competing analytics layers on top of publicly available satellite data (Landsat, Sentinel). Planet's proprietary daily-cadence data is defensible, but the analytics layer is more exposed. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet generates meaningful AI revenue through its analytics platform — the company has repositioned as a 'Space and AI' company, with AI-powered change detection, crop classification, and infrastructure monitoring products shipping to commercial and government customers [1][2]. The Tanager hyperspectral satellite data feeds directly into AI models for methane detection.

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs demonstrates genuine infinite-game qualities through its PBC charter, multi-decade satellite infrastructure investments, and mission-driven approach to Earth observation. CEO Will Marshall has led since founding in 2010, maintaining consistent purpose through the transition from NASA research project to public company. The company invests heavily in next-generation capabilities (Pelican, Tanager) even while unprofitable, prioritizing long-term data moat over near-term earnings.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Planet's purpose — illuminating environmental and social change through daily Earth imaging — is genuine, distinctive, and clearly shapes strategic decisions including the PBC charter, pro-bono disaster response, and Carbon Mapper partnership [2][3]. However, Planet was founded only in 2010 and has not yet been tested through multiple leadership transitions or prolonged market crises. The Just Cause is authentic but not yet multi-decade validated.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Planet has consistently invested in next-generation satellite capabilities (Pelican 30cm constellation, Tanager hyperspectral) despite being deeply unprofitable, choosing long-term data infrastructure over near-term margins [1]. The decision to organize as a PBC — accepting legal obligations to stakeholders beyond shareholders — was a courageous structural commitment. Maintaining heavy capital investment while guiding to $415-440M revenue demonstrates leadership willing to sacrifice short-term profitability for long-term capability.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Planet's ~945 employees generally describe a mission-driven culture with 'brilliant, kind, compassionate' colleagues, and the CEO is described as committed to transparency [2]. However, Glassdoor reviews flag scaling challenges, inconsistent middle management quality, and concerns about strategic communication to employees. Leadership stability under founder Will Marshall provides continuity, but organizational growing pains are evident.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Planet has evolved from a pure satellite imagery provider to a 'Space and AI' analytics platform, adding Tanager hyperspectral capabilities and AI-powered data products beyond basic image delivery [1][2]. This represents meaningful business model evolution. However, the core business remains satellite-based Earth observation — no transformative pivot away from the founding business model has occurred or been required yet.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Planet acknowledges competitors like Maxar and Airbus in high-resolution imaging and positions its differentiation around daily cadence and global coverage rather than 'beating' specific rivals. Management consistently frames competition as motivation to expand capabilities (Pelican for resolution, Tanager for spectral range) rather than as a zero-sum market share battle [1].

investment_thesis: Planet Labs delivered a breakout FY2026 with $307.7M revenue (26% YoY growth), Q4 revenue of $86.8M (41% YoY), and its first full year of non-GAAP profitability at $15.5M adjusted EBITDA [1]. FY2027 guidance of $415-440M implies 35-43% growth driven by Pelican high-resolution constellation deployment, Tanager hyperspectral capabilities, and expanding government contracts. The repositioning as a 'Space and AI' company adds an analytics revenue layer on top of the proprietary daily-cadence data moat. With $640M cash, $234M free cash flow generation, and the world's largest commercial satellite fleet (200+ satellites), Planet is approaching the inflection point where infrastructure investment translates to operating leverage.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on persistent GAAP losses: net loss of -$247M on $308M revenue (-80.2% profit margin) reflects massive stock-based compensation and non-cash charges that dilute shareholders [1][4]. D/E of 245% and negative ROE (-78.4%) signal balance sheet stress despite the cash position. The $17.2B market cap represents a steep 56x trailing revenue multiple for a company that has never generated GAAP profit, leaving minimal margin of safety if growth decelerates below the 35-43% guidance range. Competition from well-funded rivals (Maxar/Advent, Airbus Defence & Space, Capella Space SAR constellation) and tech giants building geospatial AI on public satellite data (Google Earth Engine, Microsoft Planetary Computer) could erode pricing power in both government and commercial markets.
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue hitting $415-440M (+35-43% growth) would confirm the AI data-intelligence business model is scaling and could drive multiple re-rating toward software/data company valuations
First full-year GAAP profitability would remove the last financial overhang and unlock institutional capital that requires profitable companies in its investment mandates
Tanager hyperspectral satellite enabling new carbon monitoring and methane detection revenue streams from ESG-focused enterprises, regulators, and carbon credit verification markets
key_risks: GAAP net loss of -$247M on $308M revenue with -80.2% profit margin — heavy stock-based compensation creates ongoing shareholder dilution that undermines per-share economics

Premium valuation at ~56x trailing revenue assumes sustained 35%+ growth — any execution miss on Pelican deployment or FY2027 guidance could trigger severe multiple compression from the $17.2B market cap

Competition from SAR providers (Capella Space, Umbra) offering all-weather day/night imaging and tech giants (Google, Microsoft) building AI analytics on freely available public satellite data threatens both government and commercial market share

Planet Labs PBC Corporate Website, Mission Statement, and AI Strategy — planet.com, accessed May 2026
